titleEn: Poultry WorldPartnerCase study: Transforming hatchery operations with WingScanJul 7Video
titleZh: WingScan自动化性别鉴定系统：意大利Capetta孵化场效率翻倍、人工减半
summaryZh: 意大利Capetta孵化场引入WingScan自动化雏鸡性别鉴定系统，成功解决劳动力短缺和人工鉴定不稳定的痛点。系统将每小时处理量从1.5-1.6万只提升至3.5-4万只，人工从10人减至4-5人（性别鉴定岗位仅需1人），工作工时从12小时缩短至6小时，错误率降至约1%。同时减少雏鸡多次人工接触，显著改善动物福利。该案例展示了自动化技术在禽业孵化环节的显著效益。
translationZh:
意大利内维的Capetta孵化场由Sergio Capetta拥有并管理，每周生产约48万只雏鸡，相当于每年约2400万只。该企业一直依赖传统的人工雏鸡性别鉴定，这一过程因劳动力短缺和结果不一致而变得越来越难以维持。

人工性别鉴定带来了严重的运营挑战。熟练劳动力越来越难找，尤其是对于这种需要精准度且常常在非正常工作时间进行的工作。Capetta解释说：“性别鉴定员的工作非常特殊。再也找不到适合做好这项工作的人员了。”

该过程也劳动密集且效率低下。生产线上需要约10名工人，经常轮班工作长达12小时。即使有这么多人手，生产力仍然有限，而且随着工人疲劳，准确性也会波动。

人工处理还引发了对雏鸡福利的额外担忧。雏鸡在过程中被多次处理，增加了应激和受伤的风险。

面对这些压力，Capetta认为变革势在必行……

**解决方案**

Capetta孵化场引入了WingScan，这是一套自动化雏鸡性别鉴定系统，旨在提高吞吐量、提升准确性、减少对人工的依赖，同时改善动物福利。

在安装时，Capetta表示：“对我的企业来说，没有这台机器就没有未来。几年后，你将找不到任何人再做人工性别鉴定。”

**实施**

WingScan的引入带来了巨大影响。不再需要10名工人进行人工性别鉴定，现在只需4-5人即可高效运行。一人将雏鸡箱装载到传送带上，三名操作员将雏鸡放置到生产线上，使机器能够连续运行。

孵化场经理Domenico Torre描述了新的设置：“只需4到5个人，我们就能处理大量雏鸡。机器以稳定的速度持续运行。”

该系统易于采用。由于其直观的界面，操作员能够快速学会如何使用。WingScan操作员Erik Sarotto指出：“与机器交互非常容易，因为它非常直观——你立刻就知道该在哪里操作。”

**结果**

生产力和劳动力效率

最直接的影响是吞吐量显著提升。产量从每小时约1.5万至1.6万只雏鸡提高到每小时高达3.5万至4万只。

同时，劳动力需求下降。“以前，生产线上大约有10个人。现在我们只有1个人，”Torre在专门指代性别鉴定岗位时说道。

工作时间减少了一半。“以前工作日持续12小时；现在只需6小时，处理相同数量的雏鸡，”Capetta说。

准确性和一致性

WingScan在性别鉴定准确性方面带来了重大改进。错误率降至约1%。“现在的性别鉴定，在我看来，是完美的，误差只有1%，”Capetta解释道。

这一改进也在农场得到了证实。Paolo Marenco分享说：“公母之间不再有失衡。”

动物福利改善

减少人工处理对雏鸡福利产生了明显的积极影响。以前，雏鸡在整个过程中被多次处理。使用WingScan后，这减少到一次转移步骤。

Marenco强调了差异：“雏鸡经历的应激比以前少得多。现在只有一个步骤，所以雏鸡被处理的次数大大减少。”

操作员和客户观察到雏鸡质量和成活率的改善。Sarotto补充道：“它们被处理的次数少得多。每个方面都得到了全面改善。”

运营可靠性和易用性

操作员一致反映系统简单可靠。界面提供清晰、实时的性能反馈，并在问题升级之前向用户发出警报。

Sarotto解释说：“机器在停止前会向你发出错误警报。干预非常及时。”

大多数问题都是小问题，几秒钟内就能解决，通常只需简单清洁。系统还捕获详细的生产数据，使操作员能够全天监控性别比例、未分类雏鸡和机器性能。

Marenco总结道：“一切都可以非常、非常简单的方式访问。任何人都可以使用它。”

服务与支持

WingScan团队的支持积极主动且可靠。安装时提供的培训使团队能够独立进行维护。

Marenco指出：“他们教我们如何处理维护，而且他们总是随时待命并提供支持。”在某些情况下，问题在生产开始前就被远程识别并迅速解决。

Capetta强化了这一观点：“他们会响应；有时他们比我们预想得更早。”

**成果**

采用WingScan从根本上改善了Capetta孵化场的运营。该企业现在以更高的生产力、更高的准确性、更低的劳动力依赖和更好的动物福利成果运营。

回顾内部收益和客户的积极反馈，Capetta说：“每个人都看到了改进。每个人！”

**结论**

通过向WingScan自动化性别鉴定过渡，Capetta孵化场解决了关键的劳动力挑战，同时在效率、质量和福利方面取得了可衡量的收益。这一举措使企业在人工性别鉴定越来越难以为继的行业中，为长期可持续发展做好了准备。
featuredReason: 该案例完整展示了自动化视觉识别系统在禽业孵化环节的落地效果，数据详实（吞吐量、人工、工时、准确率），对面临劳动力短缺的孵化场具有直接参考价值，是智慧畜牧中AI+机器视觉应用的典型范例。
contentFull:
Case study: Transforming hatchery operations with WingScan

Capetta Hatchery in Neive, Italy, which is owned and managed by Sergio Capetta, produces around 480,000 chicks each week, equating to approximately 24 million chicks annually. The business had been relying on traditional manual chick sexing, a process that was becoming increasingly difficult to sustain due to labour shortages and inconsistent results. 

Jul 7

Video

Watch the video below to see how Capetta Hatchery transformed its chick sexing process with WingScan. 

Manual sexing presented serious operational challenges. Skilled labour was becoming harder to find, especially for a role that requires precision and is often performed at unusual hours. “The job of the sexer is a very particular job,” explains Capetta. “Staff can no longer be found who are suited to do the job well.”

The process was also labour intensive and inefficient. Around 10 workers were needed on the line, often working shifts lasting up to 12 hours. Even with this level of staffing, productivity remained limited and accuracy could vary as workers became fatigued.

Manual handling raised additional concerns about chick welfare. Chicks were handled multiple times during the process, increasing stress and the risk of trauma.

Faced with these pressures, Capetta saw change as essential... 

Solution

Capetta Hatchery implemented WingScan, an automated chick sexing system designed to increase throughput, improve accuracy, and reduce reliance on manual labour while enhancing animal welfare.

On installation, Capetta said: “For my business, there is no future without this machine. In a few years you won’t find anyone doing manual sexing anymore.”

Implementation

The introduction of WingScan had a huge impact. Instead of 10 workers carrying out manual sexing, the operation now runs efficiently with 4-5 people. One person loads crates onto the conveyor while 3 operators place chicks onto the line, allowing the machine to run continuously.

Hatchery Manager Domenico Torre described the new setup: “With 4 or 5 people, we can process a large number of chicks. The machine keeps running continuously at a steady pace.”

The system proved easy to adopt. Operators were able to quickly understand how to use it, thanks to its intuitive interface. As WingScan operator Erik Sarotto noted: “Interacting with the machine is very easy because it’s very intuitive – you immediately know where to act.”

Results

Productivity and labour efficiency

The most immediate impact was a significant increase in throughput. Output rose from approximately 15,000-16,000 chicks per hour to as many as 35,000-40,000 chicks per hour.

At the same time, labour requirements dropped. “Before, on the line, there were around 10 people. Now we have 1 person,” said Torre, referring specifically to the role of manual sexing.

Working hours were reduced by half. “Before the working day lasted 12 hours; now it is 6 hours, with the same numbers of chicks,” said Capetta.

Accuracy and consistency

WingScan delivered a major improvement in sexing accuracy. Error rates fell to around 1%. “The sexing now, in my opinion, is perfect with a 1% error,” Capetta explained.

This improvement has also been confirmed on farms. Paolo Marenco shared that “there are no longer imbalances between males and females.”

Animal welfare improvements

Reducing manual handling has had a clear positive impact on chick welfare. Previously, chicks were handled multiple times throughout the process. With WingScan, that has been reduced to a single transfer step.

Marenco highlighted the difference: “Chicks experience much less trauma than before. Now there is only 1 step, so the chicks are handled much less.”

Operators and customers have observed improvements in chick quality and lower mortality rates. Sarotto added: “They’re handled much less. There has been a complete improvement in every area.”

Operational reliability and ease of use

Operators consistently report that the system is simple and reliable. The interface provides clear, real-time feedback on performance and alerts users to issues before they escalate.

Sarotto explained: “The machine alerts you to an error before it stops. Intervention is quite immediate.”

Most issues are minor and are resolved in seconds, often with simple cleaning. The system also captures detailed production data, allowing operators to monitor sex ratios, unclassified chicks, and machine performance throughout the day.

Marenco summarised the experience: “Everything is completely accessible in a very, very simple way. Anyone can use it.”

Service and support

Support from the WingScan team has been proactive and dependable. The installation included training that enabled the team to manage maintenance independently.

Marenco noted: “They taught us how to handle maintenance, and they are always present and available for support.” In some cases, issues were identified remotely before production began and were resolved quickly.

Capetta reinforced this view: “They respond; sometimes they anticipate us.”

Outcome

The adoption of WingScan has fundamentally improved operations at Capetta Hatchery. The business now operates with higher productivity, greater accuracy, reduced labour dependence, and improved animal welfare outcomes.

Reflecting both internal gains and positive feedback from customers, Capetta says: “Everyone has found an improvement. Everyone!”

Conclusion

By transitioning to automated sexing with WingScan, Capetta Hatchery has addressed critical labour challenges while achieving measurable gains in efficiency, quality, and welfare. The move has positioned the business for long-term sustainability in an industry where manual sexing is becoming increasingly difficult to maintain.
author: Targan
